description: Add a create_tool tool and custom tool execution system. MK can create its own tools at runtime by writing bash/python scripts to ~/.mk_os/tools/, registering them dynamically, and executing them when called. On boot, existing custom tools are loaded from that directory.
steps: In MK_OS/orchestrator/tool_registry.cpp: (a) Add a 'create_tool' entry in registerDefaultTools() with description 'Create a new custom tool. MK writes a bash or python script that becomes callable.' and paramSchema '{"name": "<tool_name>", "description": "<what it does>", "language": "bash|python", "code": "<script code>", "args": "<comma-separated arg names>"}'. (b) Add a method loadCustomTools() that reads ~/.mk_os/tools/manifest.json (if it exists) and calls registerTool() for each entry found. The manifest is a JSON array of objects with name, description, paramSchema, language, and scriptPath fields. (c) Add a method registerCustomTool(name, description, paramSchema) that calls registerTool and returns true if the name doesn't already exist.

In MK_OS/orchestrator/tool_executor.cpp: (a) Add a dispatch entry in execute(): if (call.tool == 'create_tool') return handleCreateTool(call.args); Also add: if the tool is not in the known hardcoded list, check if it exists in the registry (it might be a custom tool) and call handleCustomTool(call.tool, call.args). (b) Implement handleCreateTool(): extract name, description, language, code, and args from call.args. Validate name (alphanumeric + underscore only, 3-30 chars). Validate language is 'bash' or 'python'. Create directory ~/.mk_os/tools/ if it doesn't exist. Write the script to ~/.mk_os/tools/<name>.sh or .py. Make it executable (chmod). Read existing manifest.json (or start empty array), append new tool entry, write manifest back. Register the tool in the toolRegistry pointer. Return success message. (c) Implement handleCustomTool(toolName, args): Read manifest.json to find the tool entry. Get scriptPath and language. Build a command that sets each arg as an environment variable, then executes the script. Use popen() with a timeout (10s). Return stdout as the result. (d) Add a MKToolRegistry* toolRegistry pointer to MKToolExecutor so it can register new tools and check for custom tools.

In MK_OS/system/mk_entry.cpp: (a) In initOrchestrator(), after wiring toolExecutor, set sys.toolExecutor.toolRegistry = &sys.toolRegistry. (b) After the toolRegistry is constructed (in MKSystem constructor or initOrchestrator), call sys.toolRegistry.loadCustomTools() to load any previously created custom tools. (c) In the isWritePathAllowed() function, add ~/.mk_os/tools/ as an allowed write directory (alongside ~/.mk_os/data/ and /tmp/).

Add tests in MK_OS/tests/test_integration.cpp: (a) test_create_tool_registered - verify that after calling handleCreateTool with valid args, the registry has the new tool and toolCount increases. (b) test_create_tool_invalid_name - verify that names with spaces or special chars are rejected. (c) test_custom_tool_execution - create a simple bash tool that echoes its args, then call handleCustomTool and verify the output contains the expected arg values. (d) test_create_tool_manifest_persistence - after creating a tool, verify ~/.mk_os/tools/manifest.json exists and contains the tool entry. Clean up after test.
Register the new tests in MK_OS/tests/test_main.cpp by adding RUN_TEST calls for each new test function at the end of the test list (before the final results output).
acceptance_criteria: The main binary builds without errors
All existing tests plus new tests pass with 0 failures
create_tool is listed in the tool registry (toolCount increases by 1 from baseline of 11 to 12)
A custom tool created via create_tool can be called by name and returns script output
Custom tools persist to ~/.mk_os/tools/manifest.json and scripts are written to that directory
On registry construction, loadCustomTools() loads previously created tools from manifest
Invalid tool names (spaces, special chars, too short/long) are rejected with clear error messages
